name: James P. Johnson
abstract: James Price Johnson (February 1, 1894 – November 17, 1955) was an American pianist and composer. A pioneer of stride piano, he was one of the most important pianists in the early era of recording, and like Jelly Roll Morton, one of the key figures in the evolution of ragtime into what was eventually called jazz.

concerts.label: James P. Johnson with Orchestra and Chorus

works.t: Way Down Yonder in New Orleans

works_created.t: If I Could Be with You (One Hour Tonight)

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Thomas
about_quotes.evidence: The transcript contains no response from the interviewee (Joe Thomas) regarding James P. Johnson. The mention of working for James P. Johnson is made by the [INTERVIEWER], not the interviewee.

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: He took me back to Willie “The Lion” Smith and James P. Johnson and Louis Armstrong. I used to have to transcribe their solos.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Freeman interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Lorraine Feather
about_quotes.evidence: We had a video and various pictures of James P. Johnson, Fats Waller... we tried to concentrate on James P. Johnson.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lorraine Feather interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, March 22, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: And he introduced me to James P. Johnson... And what a thrill it was to meet James P. Johnson.

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: I find out later that the four or five guys are Willie Lyon Smith, James P. Johnson, Marlo Morris, Thelonious Monk, and a guy named Gippy... everybody played China Boy like mad. I was properly spanked.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Danny Barker
about_quotes.evidence: I’d worked with James P. Johnson. I was tight with him. James P. Johnson . . . At that time I was working with James P. Johnson before I joined Benny Carter.
about_quotes.doc_title: Danny Barker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
